item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The second and fourth images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the second image cannot be sourced as being released under Creative Commons, and the fourth one comes from here and is All Rights Reserved. Thus, they have been removed. The other two images come from here and here and are cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking "Oh cool, they captured the main problem and now it's (mostly) not Keter level…
…and then there was that last document…

On 11/24/2014, anomalies similar to SCP-2480 were reported in ██████, Romania. Investigation is currently ongoing.

…that's a bit unsettling for me. +1

"The battle may have been won…but the war has only just begun…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't help but notice that the 'Behemoth' creature's description, SCP-2480-2, is a dead ringer for the Shambler from Quake (1996).

I saw the silhouette of a massive humanoid, estimated to be over 4 meters in height, as it shambled out from the fog. I wanted to scream but attempted to remain calm in order to gather as much data as possible. Physical description: Its flesh was pale and flabby, its face dominated by a large, tooth filled mouth; entity lacking visible eyes, ears, or nostrils. Its teeth and three-fingered hands were heavily stained, seemingly caked in viscera

https://i.imgur.com/bWFnuvv.gif

Seems a fitting inspiration, given the overall Lovecraftian flavor of that game, and this piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Greek seems a bit Google translated to me. Would you like me to fix that?
(I was born and raised in Greece so I'd know)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super cool, an easy +1. Really strong Shadow Over Innsmouth vibes at the start, but then things started moving in an unexpected direction. Bonus points (…maybe) to referrencing harlequin babies. Ack. Just great all around, thank you so much for your work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This managed to be really good and unnerving even as a "town has a dark secret and people are acting weird" piece, which is no mean feat. But to echo others, the last tab didn't add much beyond words. Still, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Delightfully Lovercraftian, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A well-deserved +1 from me. It's reminiscent of The Shadow Over Innsmouth, as others have pointed out, but you put your own twist on the idea and turned it into something that really works well as an SCP. I would happily read a full novel based on this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The surveillance logs, day 16 specifically, seems to be referencing Akira Yamaoka's song "Hometown," from Silent Hill. Is this intentional? One of the lines is copied verbatim from the song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I for one welcome our new Sarkic Nälkä overlords.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only three upvotes away from a well-deserved +300.

There are a couple of slow parts, as mentioned above, but that doesn't detract from my overall experience. This is some good Lovecraftian horror, and I'm a big fan of anomalies that just make me sit down for a while and say "Hmmmmm…"

+1

Edit: it's there now! Congrats on the +300!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These higher dimensional SCPs always seem to attract my attention lol. Besides that +1 for giving me a mind crisis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in Addendum 2480-2 is broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I started to wonder if that was the case when no longer seeing deleted accounts among the raters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's likely because of the recent clearing of deleted account votes, which causes the article's total rating to reset to the true value with the first post-clearing vote.

TL;DR this article was probably like most others on the site and artificially inflated with deleted account upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And cancel that, today I got 4 downvotes at the same time. Well, that doesn't seem weird at all…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you everyone for my first 200. It really means a lot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact - "sitra ahra" in Aramaic is literally "the other side", true, but can also mean "Satan" or "demon". I see what you did there and I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are those people on the poles in the background of Ion's picture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know about anyone else, but I found the Surveillance Operations to be reminiscent of the video game Call of Cthulhu: Dark Corners of the Earth. I like the way it was done in this SCP better than the way that it was done in Call of Cthulhu, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effects are different besides. Mnestics enhance memory, and let you "recall the unthinkable", whereas DMT is a perception enhancer that allows users to "see the unseen". Memory and perception are obviously different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, isn't DMT just standard mnestics?

I am fairly certain that the author did not intend to include "mnestics". After all, this was written in 2014 and the first article that made use of "mnestics" is We Need To Talk About Fifty-Five which is written in 2015.

And both were written by two different users.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't really do anything for me. It has the "creepy wierd town" thing going on, but it doesn't really do anything interesting with it. And additionally, I can't really see a foundation Site Director becoming a Karcist or organizing this kind of thing.

Also, isn't DMT just standard mnestics?

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the Phillip K Dick short story where the man takes anti-hallucinogens in order to perceive the godlike creature controlling the dystopian government. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PoI-93

Do as you will

Sarkicism and Thelema? It's a strange combination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am missing something I think but why does Ion have an LTE designation, if he is still believed to be at large?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this. Can't upvote enough. Probably helps that I hadn't seen SCP-2095 or SCP-2075 before this.

It's this sort of thing that keeps me coming back to the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a description of the suits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I admit a certain fondness for exposition - the goal to create a living, seemingly real place - something of our own history and, ultimately, with dark secrets hidden from most. I would assume an actual official document would have far more than the average SCP. Again, not everyone's cup of tea I suppose.

Funny, I made sure to copy their threat analysis documents and yet I didn't consider some other details. Specifically, I'm not sure what White and Orange are in this context - which means I probably should have looked into it much more. Regardless, Psi-9 requires people with field experience as well as extensive knowledge of the occult and Counter Occult Stratagems (which I made up;p).

Nope, can't say my inspiration came from that. Also, it is a bit more complicated than what the researcher concluded. Adytum, in my mind, is now located in a sort of pocket dimension, but other iterations of Earth have entirely fallen under their sway. Thus, "Adytum" is more terrible than it ever was in the ancient past - evolving and developing on its own for over 2,000 years - and now remnant devotees are seeking to bring about its return. The cult of the Foundationverse iteration of Earth has been secretly preparing for this for a very long time. I'd go on but it would probably take pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This group are not Daevites but date to a time and place once under Daevite rule. They are an ancient cult tied to the Bronze Age Collapse but only relatively recently did they come to the Foundation's full attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait so does this tie in with SCP-2075 and if so, does that mean we are on our way to an end of the world scenario (lore wise at least)? After all I remember reading that a recurrence of the Daevite empire itself would lead to a possible extinction scenario of all human life, and with the existence of SCP-2075 there could be a force capable of causing such a scenario. Any answers on this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I ultimately upvoted, but I don't like some of the expository paragraphs (the one about the estate is the worst offender; no official document would have that in the summary material, IMO, both the history of the house and the Foundation's analysis of the cult would be extensive secondary documents on their own) and the campy early-HPL-homage dialog. Otherwise it is really good. You can write cosmic horror without aping early HPL though (if you are to copy him at least copy At the Mountains of Madness not "Reanimator", IMO). Carry on.

Edit: Some questions:

  • lDoes the GOC role in in Psi-9 include the deployment of White and Orange suits?
  • Is the alternate past intruding on the present inspired by Mary Gentle's Ash or Ken Hite's GURPS Horror: The Madness Dossier?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm new, and only read the Daevite story like a week or two ago, so I don't really know how I feel about it tying in, but I guess it must be a problem because the author felt that it was accurate enought to just say "Daevites" and not even list the SCP number. Do we have a Daevite tag so that I can see which ones are crossreferenced? Or is it just a site-search

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, some art by SunnyParralax related to 2480:

+ show block
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha! You caught it. I'm a big fan of VNV. Dr. Narvaez has been fun. Actually based on some real researchers who did research on hallucinogenics through using them on themselves. Didn't remotely stunt their intelligence or ability to do research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A VNV Nation quote? Really? I wonder if the doctor or agent who wrote the MTF's mission statement in-universe is a fan.
I like what you did with the DMT. Dr. Narvaez is all kinds of awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. +1
Raymond Chandler plus Lovecraft plus SCP canon plus superb writing equals a really exemplary article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! Missed that; corrected it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed a couple typos under the "Experimental Research" collapsible. At one point it says self-inflected, when I think you mean self-inflicted. It also includes perfunctoriness, the noun form, when it should just be perfunctory, the adjective modifying malaise. Unless these are intentional. Either way, great writing and upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This summarizes my thoughts pretty well — its Lovecraftian nature is both a strong point and my second biggest hesitation, the first biggest hesitation being the about-face in tone in the last collapsible and a lot of extraneous information there.

Very well written and engaging, but the last collapsible honestly does ruin the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Intended; the location that inspired this is my hometown, which was neighbors with Innsmouth in the story;p Meant to be an homage of sorts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The surveillance mission logs kind of reminded me of The Shadow Over Innsmouth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral voting. The majority of this is brilliant writing, to the point where as the article kept going, I was dissuaded from my initial inclination to downvote the hell out of it for being another Lovecraft-retread, of which we have quite enough around here. The setting is convincingly laid out, the supplemental materials helped sell this as a group of real people caught in something laughably beyond their comprehension, and the final experiment was wonderfully terrifying.

The last addendum, though, really drags this down. You spent a lot of time writing something that was compelling enough on its own, enough so that it more than made up for the well-worn tropes employed in getting it where it needed to go. But instead of letting this rightfully stand on its own, you then tie it in to other works on the site without nearly the artifice and work that you put in to make the horrifying town you created what it was. The "oh I guess it's Daevites" part of it almost reached "probably memetic or something" levels. It could have ended before you got to that addendum, and I would have upvoted while feeling there could have been more. A proper ending to this would make it one of the better articles on the site. But as it stands, that last part is extremely discordant to the rest of the piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have but 1 upvote to give. Really shouldn't have read this at 1 in the morning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe the idea is that what we're seeing is a scanned document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm just not cool yet;p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple of minor problems : "as quarantine or relocation of inhabitants have been deemed inoperative." This just doesn't sound right. Maybe "has been deemed infeasible", or "has been deemed inappropriate/impossible"?

I'm not a fan of strike through in what is meant to be a maintained computer record. Is it really necessary to retain incomplete or incorrect information like this?

I'm also not sure what give it "presumed neutralised" status when the inhabitants of the town are still affected by it, and the suggestion is that you can still see it with DMT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This really had me interested in the beginning. Sure, depraved cults and otherworldly horrors are no strangers to this site, but all the strange little details made me truly curious, and eager to read on…

+Spoilers
-It was his sled

Once I realized that this was being tied into SCP-2510, which I wasn't a fan of, I couldn't help but feel a little disappointed. Then it became apparent that you were tying this into even more familiar territory, and that's where you lost me. Quite frankly, I'm not all that interested in this kind of retreading/reimagining of old site lore. That's just me, though.

…You certainly do know how to put a story together, though. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish this had more upvotes, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing slays, Meta. I really enjoyed reading it. +7

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So much +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's almost like the Daevites should be their own GoI now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's with the increased appearance of Daevite manifestations in the SCPverse close to or at the present day? Thanks for skewing my headcanon in a very scary direction. Also, great writing and the Site Director's image was quite scary. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I once dated an Innsmouth girl…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chatted up a cute native. Loosened her lips with a few drinks. Got nothing of value, at least with regards to information.

"at least with regards to information"

…he got her number, didn't he.

…he's dating the girl from the interdimensional eldritch horror-town now, isn't he.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Every backwater where the locals give you a nasty look…

Might just be a colony and their domesticated (baseline) humans;p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured that much, but you make an excellent point I hadn't considered. We'd just piss them off by destroying their "port". At best it's a Roanoke-style situation and they come back in a place we don't find out about until it's just as bad or worse as it is in the town.

Also, I have a feeling that their second 'landing' was not far from Arefu, was it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually thought about that a bit! The entities are bleeding into this reality at that known location (in reality, it is likely occurring in many places). In truth, they aren't *fully* manifested here - think of the realm seen as a sort of "border". Actually, I was originally going to call this "The Border War" because of that;p

In appearances, it would end up looking like a random town in Massachusetts was nuked;p Think of these things as colonizers; our world is an island, with little means of traveling across a vast sea but these invaders have come from another land, attempt to colonize it, but the natives (us) ultimately kill them off. There is so so so many more on the other side;p and there are people on our side, quite willing to betray their own for the promises they offer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I love all of this.

I just have one question.

Given the nature of this, why hasn't the Foundation decided to attempt a nuclear detonation? Not in the city(well, not the city in our world) but in the portions that are somewhere/when else?

I mean, we can pass through the barriers, but that seems to rely on the ability to perceive it. An explosion perceives nothing.

Excellent none the less, just a thought I had when it all started to click.
